answer:
In Mirza Ghalib's *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, the heart (*dil*) is a central motif, often embodying vulnerability, longing, and existential flux. One couplet portrays it as profoundly vital and precious, penetrated to its innermost core by the beloved's glance:
Your glance passed from my heart into my core;
With a single charm, their will to fight is gone.[^1]
In stark contrast, another depicts the heart as casually disposable, freely bestowed despite the inevitability of loss and betrayal:
I gave my heart; she’s human, prone to err... what is there to say?
My rival is the one she sends, her courier... what is there to say?[^2]
The first (*ghazal* 35) underscores the heart's depth and sanctity—its "core" (*jigar*) implies an irreplaceable essence, yielding only to transcendent beauty.[^3] The second (*ghazal* 37) treats it as expendable currency in love's futile economy, handed over with resigned fatalism amid human frailty.[^4] This duality reflects Ghalib's mastery: the heart as both cherished sanctum and transient offering. [^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/35-dil-se-teri-nigaah-jigar-tak-utar-gayi/translation/#p-4ab0d4). [^2]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/37-diya-hai-dil-agar-uss-ko-bashar-hai-kya-kahiye/translation/#p-ddcc4b). [^3]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 35 (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/35-dil-se-teri-nigaah-jigar-tak-utar-gayi/translation/). [^4]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 37 (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/37-diya-hai-dil-agar-uss-ko-bashar-hai-kya-kahiye/translation/).
